text: The surgeon performs a vagal sparing maneuver by identifying the vagal trunk and accessing the lesser sac between the vagal trunk and the stomach. The gastric pouch is tailored using an Endo-GIA stapler from the right upper quadrant, rotated clockwise for anterior lip formation. A calibration tube is inserted, and the stapler is used from the left mid quadrant port aiming towards the left crus area. An SFGL retractor is placed, and vertical buttressed staplers are used from the left upper quadrant port at Palmer's point to complete the pouch.

The surgeon splits the omentum and identifies the ligament of Treitz, measuring approximately seventy-five to one hundred centimeters to form the omega loop. A Penrose drain is applied, and the omega loop is lifted adjacent to the gastric pouch area to begin tailoring the gastrojejunostomy (GJ). The assistant or surgeon holds the barrel to prevent twisting of the omega loop.
The surgeon places an anterior outer stitch using a 22 cm Vicryl 2-0 dyed suture, followed by a posterior outer layer stitch of the same type and length.

The surgeon takes bites of tissue from the posterior wall, avoiding the staple line to preserve blood supply, and then moves anteriorly near the staple line. The posterior layer is started on the vertical stapled area approximately two centimeters from the edge.

The surgeon locks the last stitch and transects the omega loop using a white load from the left mid quadrant port. Two or three bites are taken to split the mesentery, facilitating easier JJ reconstruction. The edictube is advanced, and a gastrotomy is performed using an ultrasonic dissector, creating an incision about 1.5 centimeters long. An enterotomy is then dilated, followed by the start of the posterior inner layer suturing, using a 22-centimeter suture.

The surgeon continues suturing from the bowel side, taking all layers with each bite until reaching the corner. Each stitch is tightened carefully, with particular attention to the corner stitches to prevent leaks.

The surgeon proceeds with suturing the anterior inner layer using a 17-centimeter long suture, carefully avoiding the posterior wall and layers from both sides. The mucosa is tucked in, and the edict tube is passed to prevent piercing the string while tying the posterior inner layer to the anterior inner layer.
The surgeon carefully sutures the anterior inner layer, ensuring no bites are taken from the posterior wall. The inner row of sutures is hidden, and each bite is tightened progressively.

The surgeon ties the posterior outer layer stitch and performs a leak test under saline immersion. The Roux limb is measured counterclockwise to 150 centimeters and sutured to the PP-limb using a 25-centimeter long ether bond. A silk suture is placed for the assistant, and the orientation of the Roux limb is checked from the JJ area back to the GJ area to ensure the mesentery is not twisted before proceeding with enterotomies.

The surgeon creates enterotomies approximately one centimeter away from the stitch in a diagonal manner, spreading longitudinally along the bowel axis to avoid large openings. Using a wide 60mm endo-GIA from the right upper quadrant port, the surgeon closes the enterotomies while withdrawing to prevent widening. The common enterotomy is closed with a 20cm 2-0 Vicryl suture in a running fashion, holding the common channel ruling area initially and then the Ethibond suture near the crotch, inverting it inwards as shown.

The surgeon inverts the tissue inwards and ties it to the Ethibond tail, then proceeds with defect closure using Ethibond sutures. The surgeon moves up along the Roux limb and the BP limb, ensuring the common channel Roux limb connection is not narrowed at the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) site.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect by piercing the base of the tissue and tightening the suture.
The surgeon closes the Peterson defect using two figure-of-eight sutures, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
